title: Our coding struggles.
author: StockGPTpro
selftext:
After testing a lot of architectures for stock prediction, we started arriving at a slightly uncomfortable conclusion:

A huge percentage of retail “AI trading systems” are just momentum strategies with extra abstraction layered on top.



Not saying that as criticism either.



Momentum is one of the few effects that has persisted across decades and across markets.



The issue is that a lot of apparent “AI alpha” starts disappearing once you properly control for:

\- factor exposure

\- sector concentration

\- beta drift

\- volatility clustering

\- turnover

\- regime dependence



We found that many models which initially looked impressive were effectively just:

\- long high beta growth

\- long earnings momentum

\- or short volatility without explicitly realizing it



Especially during strong trend environments.



What became more interesting to us wasn’t:

“Can the model predict returns?”



but:

“Can the model improve cross-sectional ranking quality after stripping out obvious factor effects?”



That turned out to be much harder than expected.



A few things we noticed:

\- adding model complexity rarely helped as much as improving signal orthogonality

\- many alternative data features added noise rather than incremental information

\- ensemble diversification mattered more than maximizing single-model Sharpe

\- feature stability across regimes became more important than peak backtest performance



The deeper we got into it, the more it felt like the real challenge wasn’t building an AI model.



It was avoiding accidental factor bets disguised as intelligence.



Curious how many people here building live systems have run into the same issue.

comments.author: No-Consequence-1779
comments.body:
Ultimately, the LLM will need to use or appear to use the existing formulas for the op stated above strategies. An LLM absolutely does not provide some advantage- it adds latency for the api call. 

This is why hft do not use llms.  It’s absurd to anyone who slightly understands it. 

It is or can be effective marketing material for the intellectually impaired.  Let them be …

replies.author: StockGPTpro
replies.body:
Completely agree that LLMs themselves aren’t some magical source of alpha.

And agreed that HFT firms aren’t using LLM inference in execution loops — latency alone makes that unrealistic.

Our point was more that a lot of retail “AI trading” projects market themselves as novel intelligence when they’re often just rediscovering known factors with extra abstraction layered on top.

The actual challenge for us has been:

\- signal selection

\- cross-sectional ranking quality

\- regime robustness

\- avoiding hidden factor concentration

\- and preventing the system from collapsing into a disguised momentum strategy

We’re also not using LLMs for high-frequency execution or trying to compete with HFT infrastructure.

The interesting use case for us has been around:

\- ranking interpretation

\- unstructured information processing

\- and improving research workflows around the core ranking engine

Think the broader point is that a lot of people conflate:

“uses AI”

with

“has edge”

when most of the hard problems in systematic trading still come down to data quality, signal robustness, and portfolio construction.

replies.author: No-Consequence-1779
replies.body:
I’m primarily an GenAI engineer and software developer. I’ve done a lot of crypto trading. 

My gains for equities are solely information based. An example is late last year, MU and others announced they were halting consumer production to focus on enterprise /data centers (this was obvious to me seeing the second hand maker double and now 4x) at that time this was all about ai. 

So I went in heavy. There was zero doubt of the outcome. The freaking fabs were already sold out years in advance. Some others like rocket labs and then storage. Quite a few others because I understand the Supplychain and market completely. 
Simple and maybe lucky. 

For crypto, since prediction is not possible, the best use I could see was real time monitoring to ride the valley to peak. Yes it worked. But it was essentially doing the same math. 

We all know most traders are not profitable. And it can be an addiction like gambling. 

You’re absolutely right on the information collection / research and identification workflows. This is an effect use. And with self modifying software driven by LLMs , the adaptation is on the fly. 

I’ve recently been putting together this workflow for fcc filings to follow the insider decisions of trading by execs. Highly effective so far. Already been done a thousand times I’m sure.

comments.author: OldHobbitsDieHard
comments.body: What did you expect it to do?

comments.author: mdawe1
comments.body: Its been ok in evaluating current open positions for potential displacements by stocks that pass the gates, but even then its not a huge advantage relative to do the same thing via simple score displacement.
